body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes (opencode has a config-driven per-call permission classifier: allow / ask / deny, with bash wildcard patterns)
daemon_capability: full (safe-path arc readyâ†’workingâ†’ready, no waiting â€” proven observable on the ProcessOwnedStore transport)
driver_capability: ready (the SAFE branch this cell asserts needs NO interactive approval â€” it is config-auto-classified, so the prior gap:permission-approval no longer applies)

## What this cell asserts

auto-classified-permission is the SAFE-PATH branch of permission classification: a benign tool call is AUTO-APPROVED without firing a permission prompt, and the session traces `ready â†’ working â†’ ready`, never entering `waiting`. The risky-path branch (the prompt actually firing â†’ `waiting`) is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell. The claudecode sibling's expected.jsonl pins exactly this: a `no_waiting_window` invariant across the whole turn with `no state_transition to waiting`.

## Re-derivation (this reassessment): the driver gap is NOT relevant to the safe branch

The prior verdict (partial / full / gap:permission-approval) treated opencode's classifier as TUI-only and concluded the safe path was unrecordable headlessly. Re-deriving against opencode v1.14.50 + settings_file_support overturns the driver-gap half for THIS cell:

1. opencode's permission system IS config-driven (settings_file_support:true). The `permission` block in opencode.json keys tools (`bash`, `edit`, `read`, `webfetch`, â€¦) to `"allow"` / `"ask"` / `"deny"`, and `bash` supports per-command wildcard patterns (`"git *": "allow"`, `"git commit *": "deny"`). This is a real AUTO-classifier: a per-call safe-vs-risky decision driven by static config, NOT only the TUI overlay. (opencode.ai/docs/permissions/.)
2. For the SAFE branch a benign tool set to `"allow"` (or matching an allow-wildcard) AUTO-EXECUTES with NO prompt â†’ `ready â†’ working â†’ ready`, never `waiting`. v1.14.50 also ships `opencode run --dangerously-skip-permissions` ("auto-approve permissions that are not explicitly denied"), confirmed in `opencode run --help` on the installed binary â€” a headless YOLO auto-classify-approve that needs no interactive answer at all.
3. Therefore the safe path is fully recordable on the EXISTING headless driver: `drive-opencode-interactive.sh` already implements `send` + `wait_turn` over `opencode run`, and the auto-classified-approve needs only a settings file carrying an `allow` permission policy (or the `--dangerously-skip-permissions` flag). NO approve/deny/classify driver step is required for this branch. The old `gap:permission-approval` was the RISKY-branch primitive (answering an interactive prompt); it does not gate the safe branch.

## daemon side (daemon_capability: full)

opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter (core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go). The watcher (watcher.go) reads `part` rows and emits EventNewSession / EventActivity / EventRemoved; the parser (parser.go) maps tool `status=pending|running` â†’ `function_call` (working), `status=completed|error` â†’ `function_call_output`, and `step-finish reason=stop` â†’ `turn_done` (ready). The ungated readyâ†’workingâ†’ready arc with the no-waiting invariant is exactly what this transport already observes â€” PROVEN by opencode/auto-executed-tool-call (yes/full/ready), whose `write` tool ran inside the working span with zero waiting/approval state. So the daemon emits the safe-path arc this cell requires today.

## The honest caveats (why confidence 0.8, not higher)

- The config `allow` (or `--dangerously-skip-permissions`) is a STATIC policy lookup, not the in-product per-call classifier surfacing a discriminating decision. The resulting recording is byte-for-byte INDISTINGUISHABLE from auto-executed-tool-call (a generic ungated tool call). It proves the safe-path invariant (`readyâ†’workingâ†’ready`, no waiting) but does NOT separately prove an auto-CLASSIFICATION step occurred.
- The classifier's discriminating (SELECTIVE-prompt) behavior is the RISKY half and is unobservable headlessly: a tool set to `"ask"` HANGS FOREVER in headless/server mode â€” the tool part stays at `status=running` and the awaiting-input Promise never resolves (GitHub anomalyco/opencode #14473, #16367). The daemon would then see perpetual `working`, never `waiting` (the ProcessOwnedStore transport has no `waiting`/permission source at all). That half is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell, which stays gap:permission-approval until a live-REPL driver can ANSWER the prompt.

## Routing

Record the safe-path arc via a settings file with an `allow` permission policy (or `--dangerously-skip-permissions`) on the existing headless driver, with the explicit caveat that the recording demonstrates the no-waiting safe-path invariant, NOT a separately-observable classification step. The maintainer should weigh whether this adds signal beyond auto-executed-tool-call; if not, the cell can stay frozen-by-choice, but the technical blocker (the driver gap) is gone for the safe branch.

## Confidence

0.8: the config permission schema (allow/ask/deny + bash wildcards) and the `--dangerously-skip-permissions` flag are read directly from opencode docs and the installed v1.14.50 `opencode run --help`; the headless `"ask"` hang is sourced to two open GitHub issues; the daemon's safe-path observability is proven by the sibling auto-executed-tool-call recording and read directly from parser.go/watcher.go. Not higher because the recording cannot separately demonstrate the CLASSIFICATION discriminator (the cell's nominal distinguishing signal) on the headless path â€” it can only demonstrate the no-waiting safe invariant, which auto-executed-tool-call already covers.
caveats: Driver gap REMOVED for the safe branch: opencode v1.14.50 auto-classifies via config (permission.bash/edit/â€¦ = allow/ask/deny, bash wildcards) and ships `opencode run --dangerously-skip-permissions` â€” the safe arc auto-executes with NO prompt, so the existing send/wait_turn headless driver records it without any approve/deny step. The prior gap:permission-approval was the RISKY-branch primitive and does not gate this cell.

The safe-path recording is indistinguishable from auto-executed-tool-call (yes/full/ready): a config `allow` is a static policy lookup, not a separately-observable per-call classifier. It proves the `readyâ†’workingâ†’ready` no-waiting invariant, NOT that an auto-CLASSIFICATION step fired. Maintainer should decide whether it adds signal beyond auto-executed-tool-call.

The classifier's SELECTIVE (risky) half is unobservable headlessly: `"ask"` hangs forever in headless mode (tool part stuck at status=running, Promise never resolves â€” GitHub #14473, #16367), so the daemon would see perpetual working, never waiting. That is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell, which remains gap:permission-approval until a live-REPL driver can answer a prompt.

The ProcessOwnedStore transport has no `waiting`/permission source for opencode at all â€” the canonical rule-0 PermissionPendingâ†’waiting gate is a claudecode-hook path opencode's store does not feed; opencode's state arc is derived purely from part types (tool status + step-finish reason).

sources.note: permission config keys (bash/edit/read/webfetch/â€¦) accept allow/ask/deny; bash supports per-command wildcard patterns ("git *": "allow", "git commit *": "deny") â€” a real config-driven per-call auto-classifier, set via the settings file (settings_file_support:true)

PROOF the risky half is unobservable headlessly: a bash permission set to "ask" hangs forever in headless/server mode â€” the tool call enters status=running and never completes; the awaiting-input Promise never resolves and no recoverable permission event is emitted. (Corroborated by #16367 for opencode serve+attach.)

headless path implements send + wait_turn over `opencode run`; a settings file with a permission `allow` policy (or --dangerously-skip-permissions, confirmed in `opencode run --help` v1.14.50) makes the safe call auto-execute with no prompt â€” NO approve/deny/classify step needed for the safe branch, so driver_capability:ready here

tool status pending/running â†’ function_call (working), completed/error â†’ function_call_output, step-finish reason=stop â†’ turn_done (ready); NO permission/ask/classify/waiting branch â€” the daemon can emit the safe-path readyâ†’workingâ†’ready no-waiting arc but has no waiting source to represent a gated call

ProcessOwnedStore watcher reads `part` rows â†’ EventNewSession/EventActivity/EventRemoved; no permission/waiting concept â€” a headless "ask" hang would surface as a tool part stuck at status=running (perpetual working), never a waiting transition

PROOF the daemon observes the ungated safe arc on this transport (yes/full/ready): gemma's `write` tool ran inside the working span with zero waiting/approval state (readyâ†’workingâ†’ready, no waiting). A safe-path auto-classified-permission recording would be indistinguishable from it.

sibling risky-path branch â€” stays gap:permission-approval (the interactive-answer primitive headless opencode lacks). This cell (the safe branch) and that one are the two halves of opencode's permission classifier; only the safe half is recordable without a live-REPL approval driver.
